metadata.summary: Framework for choosing LLM enhancement strategy: prompting, RAG, fine-tuning, or RAFT. Score prompting first before investing in retrieval or training infrastructure.

content.title: LLM Decision Framework
content.summary: A systematic framework for choosing the right LLM enhancement strategy — prompt engineering, RAG, fine-tuning, or RAFT (hybrid) — based on data freshness, accuracy requirements, budget, latency, and team constraints. Always score prompting first before investing in retrieval or training infrastructure.

why.p: Without a structured decision process, teams default to RAG or fine-tuning prematurely and waste infrastructure budget. The framework enforces the correct evaluation order: prompting (free, immediate) → RAG (medium investment) → fine-tuning (high investment) → RAFT (highest investment). It also provides a scoreable decision matrix that an agent can apply programmatically to produce a structured recommendation with rationale.

list.item: At the start of any AI feature design: choose the right architecture before writing code
Evaluating whether to augment an existing LLM feature
During cost optimization: deciding whether a fine-tuned cheaper model can replace an expensive general model
When accuracy is inadequate: diagnosing if the root cause is knowledge, style, or reasoning
Business justification for AI budget: quantify cost/benefit before committing
Prototype needed in under a day — default to prompt engineering, evaluate later
Decision already made by stakeholders — use the framework to document trade-offs, not re-litigate
Task is purely generative (creative writing) — retrieval and fine-tuning do not apply
No eval data exists — the framework requires measurable accuracy targets; skip until measurable

rule.statement: Always evaluate approaches in this order: prompt engineering first, then RAG, then fine-tuning, then RAFT. Never skip directly to fine-tuning without measuring baseline accuracy with prompting.
rule.rationale: Each step up the hierarchy adds infrastructure cost and operational burden. Prompt engineering is free and reversible; fine-tuning costs thousands of dollars and locks in a data snapshot. Measuring baseline accuracy first prevents over-engineering.

rule.statement: Use RAG when data changes more often than monthly, volume exceeds 100 documents, or citations are required. Use fine-tuning when output format must be exact, domain vocabulary is specialized, and 500+ labeled examples exist.
rule.rationale: RAG provides freshness; fine-tuning provides format and style consistency. These are complementary, not competing — RAFT combines both when maximum accuracy is required and budget/expertise allows.

section.name: Cost Matrix (2026)
section.p: Setup and ongoing cost ranges by approach:

list.item: Prompt engineering: $0 setup, $0.01-0.10/1K tokens ongoing, hours to production
Structured output: $0 setup, +10-20% tokens vs baseline, hours to production
RAG: $500-5K setup, $100-2K/month infrastructure + tokens, days to weeks
Fine-tuning: $1K-50K setup, 2-6x inference cost vs base model, weeks to months
RAFT (hybrid): $5K-100K setup, full infrastructure cost, months

rule.statement: Calculate fine-tuning break-even as: (fine-tuning cost) / (savings per 1M tokens * volume). If break-even exceeds 6 months, choose RAG instead.
rule.rationale: Fine-tuning ROI calculations typically ignore retraining cost on data drift. At low-to-medium query volumes, RAG is almost always cheaper even when fine-tuning quality is marginally higher.

rule.statement: Define accuracy metrics before choosing an approach. If you cannot measure accuracy, you cannot apply the framework — collect eval data first.
rule.rationale: Teams applying this framework without eval data produce subjective recommendations that get reversed when metrics become available. "Good enough" without measurement leads to systematic over-engineering.

list.item: Accuracy target: factual correctness; >95% required for production
Latency target: <2s for user-facing; <500ms for cached RAG
Cost per query: <$0.01 for high-volume applications
Data freshness: real-time requires RAG; static knowledge allows fine-tuning
Hallucination rate: <1% for safety-critical applications

rule.statement: Assess data freshness, volume, and sensitivity before evaluating any technical approach; these three factors eliminate at least one or two options before reaching budget or team constraints.
rule.rationale: Daily-changing data eliminates fine-tuning as the primary approach. Fewer than 100 documents eliminates RAG. On-premise data residency eliminates cloud-only managed services. Early elimination saves weeks of work on approaches that were never viable.

list.item: Data freshness: daily/hourly → RAG required; monthly → RAG or periodic fine-tuning; rarely/never → fine-tuning viable
Data volume: <100 documents → prompt engineering with context; 100-10K → RAG; 10K+ → RAG with advanced retrieval
Data sensitivity: public cloud OK → any approach; private cloud only → self-hosted models + RAG; on-premise only → local models, on-prem vector DB
Latency: <500ms → prompt engineering or cached RAG; 500ms-2s → RAG acceptable; >2s → any approach
Accuracy: general quality → prompt engineering; high accuracy with sources → RAG; domain-specific precision → fine-tuning
Citations required: yes → RAG (provides source documents); no → any approach
Output format: flexible → prompt engineering; consistent JSON/XML → structured output; domain-specific format → fine-tuning
Scale: <1K queries/day → any approach; 1K-100K → cost-optimize with caching; >100K → fine-tuned smaller models
Setup budget: <$1K → prompt engineering only; $1K-10K → RAG viable; $10K-50K → fine-tuning viable; >$50K → full stack including RAFT
Monthly budget: <$100 → prompt engineering + caching; $100-1K → basic RAG; $1K-10K → production RAG or fine-tuning
ML expertise: none → prompt engineering, managed services; basic → RAG with existing tools; advanced → fine-tuning
Timeline: days → prompt engineering only; weeks → RAG; months → fine-tuning

rule.statement: Re-evaluate the decision after 3 months in production: LLM provider pricing drops 50-70% annually and the cost-quality frontier shifts regularly.
rule.rationale: A fine-tuning decision made on 2025 pricing may not hold in 2026 when the base model quality improves enough to match the fine-tuned output at standard pricing. Document the decision with current pricing in an ADR for easy future revisit.

example.title: Customer support: 500 articles, weekly updates (RAG)
example.code:
Decision factors: data_freshness: weekly → RAG viable data_volume: 500 articles → RAG appropriate citations_required: yes → RAG required latency: less than 2s → RAG acceptable volume: 10K/day → add caching
Architecture: OpenAI ada-002 → Qdrant → Cohere reranker → GPT-4o Monthly cost: ~$850 (vector DB $200 + embeddings $50 + reranker $100 + LLM $500)

example.title: Legal: static contracts, strict JSON output
example.code:
Decision factors: data_freshness: static → fine-tuning OK output_format: strict JSON → structured output + fine-tuning domain_expertise: high → fine-tuning beneficial accuracy: greater than 98% → fine-tuning + evaluation volume: 100/day → cost not primary concern
Architecture: PDF parser → fine-tuned GPT-4o → JSON schema validation Setup: $5,000 (1,000 annotated contracts, OpenAI SFT) Monthly: $300 (100 docs * 10K tokens)

example.title: Internal docs: 2000 pages, low budget
example.code:
Decision factors: data_volume: 2,000 pages → basic RAG privacy: internal only → self-hosted or private cloud budget: low → open-source stack complexity: simple → managed solutions
Architecture: Confluence sync → Chroma (file-based) → GPT-4o-mini → Slack bot Monthly cost: ~$150 ($50 infra + $100 LLM)

example.title: Medical: maximum accuracy and compliance
example.code:
Decision factors: data_volume: 10M+ papers → advanced RAG required citations: required → RAG essential accuracy: maximum → RAFT justified regulation: FDA → full documentation and audit
Architecture: Medical NER (fine-tuned) → Multi-index RAG (PubMed + guidelines) → Fine-tuned medical LLM → Differential diagnosis + citations → Human expert review
Setup: $50,000 (data prep + fine-tuning + infra) Monthly: $10,000 ($5K infra + $3K LLM + $2K compliance) Note: RAFT is only justified here because simpler approaches demonstrably cannot meet the accuracy and citation requirements.

antipattern.description: Recommending RAFT (RAG + fine-tuning hybrid) as the first option for a new feature.
antipattern.reason: RAFT requires continuous retraining infrastructure most teams lack. It is theoretically optimal but operationally fragile. Start with RAG alone; add fine-tuning only when RAG alone cannot meet the accuracy bar after tuning.

antipattern.description: Choosing RAFT for a customer support chatbot with 500 articles.
antipattern.reason: RAG alone meets all requirements at 1/10th the cost. RAFT adds fine-tuning infrastructure, retraining cycles, and operational complexity with no accuracy benefit when the knowledge base is small and well-structured.

rule.statement: Always run the "start with prompting" step first and measure baseline accuracy before investing in RAG or fine-tuning.
rule.rationale: Baseline establishes if the problem is solvable at all without expensive infrastructure.

rule.statement: Define accuracy metrics before choosing an approach — "good enough" without measurement leads to over-engineering.
rule.rationale: Measurable targets prevent subjective decisions that get reversed later.

rule.statement: RAG vs fine-tuning is not either/or: use RAG for knowledge, fine-tuning for style/format, RAFT for both.
rule.rationale: They solve different problems and often complement each other.

rule.statement: For fine-tuning ROI: calculate break-even at (fine-tuning cost) / (savings per 1M tokens * volume) — often >6 months.
rule.rationale: ROI analysis prevents uneconomical fine-tuning investments.

rule.statement: Re-evaluate the decision after 3 months: cost/quality curves change as LLM providers compete.
rule.rationale: Quarterly review ensures the recommendation stays aligned with market changes.

rule.statement: Document the decision in an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) with the scoring rationale — avoids revisiting without data.
rule.rationale: ADRs create institutional memory and prevent re-litigating decisions.

rule.statement: Test RAG before fine-tuning: RAG is reversible and often matches fine-tuning accuracy at lower cost for knowledge tasks.
rule.rationale: RAG is the lower-risk first option for knowledge-based problems.

rule.statement: Agents applying this framework tend to default to RAG for everything — prompt them to score prompting first.
rule.rationale: Agents need explicit instruction to follow the evaluation order.

rule.statement: The framework output (a recommendation) should be a human-in-loop checkpoint: agent proposes, human approves before infra is provisioned.
rule.rationale: Major architecture decisions should never be fully automated.

rule.statement: Cost estimates must use current provider pricing — training an agent on stale cost data produces wrong recommendations.
rule.rationale: Pricing changes rapidly; agent prompts must include current rates.

rule.statement: RAFT recommendation from an agent is almost always premature — it requires continuous retraining infra that most teams lack.
rule.rationale: Agents should be prompted to recommend RAFT only when simpler approaches fail eval on multiple metrics.

rule.statement: When the agent recommends fine-tuning, validate that sufficient labeled data exists (≥1000 examples) before proceeding.
rule.rationale: Fine-tuning without sufficient data produces degraded models.

rule.statement: A meta-agent applies this framework at the planning stage of a feature. It takes a requirements document as input, scores each approach against the decision matrix (data freshness, accuracy target, cost budget, latency requirement), and outputs a structured recommendation with rationale. The recommendation is then used to configure downstream subagents.
rule.rationale: Systematic scoring produces defensible decisions. For iterative improvement, the meta-agent monitors live metrics and re-triggers the decision framework when accuracy or cost thresholds drift beyond acceptable bounds.

template.purpose: Scoring function that outputs recommendation given FeatureRequirements dataclass
Architecture Decision Record template for documenting LLM enhancement choice
Prompt for LLM-assisted requirements extraction and approach recommendation

see-also.link: RAFT: Adapting Language Model to Domain Specific RAG
